title: Why Indications Form: Appearance, Timing, and Persistence
narrationScript: By the time developer goes on, the part has already answered every question. This lesson teaches you to read the reply. An indication is not the flaw itself. It is penetrant bleeding back out of a trapped reservoir, blotted and spread by the developer into a visible mark. The size and shape of that reservoir control everything. Know the material first. Castings hide cold shuts, shrinkage, and porosity. Rolled and forged stock carries seams and laps. Grinding and heat treating add their own cracks, and service adds fatigue. Then read four things together. Shape first. Jagged lines say crack, smooth curves say cold shut, straight lines say seam, and rounded spots say porosity. Strength next. More trapped penetrant means a wider, brighter mark. Then speed. Open flaws bloom the moment the developer dries, while tight deep cracks build slowly through the development window. Persistence is the last check. Wipe a suspect indication and watch. A flaw with real depth refills and redevelops. A shallow condition stays quiet. Appearance, timing, and persistence identify the discontinuity, and identification always comes before evaluation.

sections.heading: The Indication Is the Messenger
sections.body:
By the developer station the physics is finished: penetrant entered every surface-breaking flaw during dwell, removal stripped the excess, and the developer is now blotting trapped penetrant back out. What you see is therefore **not the discontinuity** — it is **bleed-out**, a mark many times wider than the opening that fed it.

Every flaw behaves as a **reservoir with a mouth**. Capillary action filled it; the developer reverses the flow and drains it. Three properties of that reservoir govern everything an indication can tell you: the **volume** of penetrant it traps, the **width of its mouth** at the surface, and its **depth**. A wide, shallow scratch traps almost nothing and gives a faint mark that quickly stalls. A tight, deep fatigue crack traps a surprising amount and feeds the developer for the entire development period.

That is why a Level II reads four things together — **shape**, **strength**, **speed of appearance**, and **persistence** — and why interpretation, deciding what produced the indication, always comes before evaluation, deciding whether the part meets its acceptance criteria.

sections.heading: Know the Material Before You Read the Part
sections.body:
Reading indications starts before the booth light comes on: the material and its processing history tell you which discontinuities can exist, where they live, and what shapes to expect.

- **Castings** — cold shuts where two metal fronts met too cold to fuse; hot tears at section changes; gas porosity; shrinkage, from gross cavities down to spongy **microshrinkage**.
- **Rolled, drawn, and extruded stock** — seams running straight in the working direction; laminations breaking out at cut edges; inclusion stringers opened by machining.
- **Forgings** — laps folded in by the dies; bursts, which are usually internal and reach PT only if machining exposes them.
- **Secondary processing** — quench cracks starting at fillets, keyways, holes, and section changes; grinding checks in fine, sharp patterns, typically at right angles to the grinding direction or in networks; machining tears.
- **Service** — fatigue cracks at stress risers; stress-corrosion cracking, often branched; corrosion pitting.

PT answers for only the surface-breaking members of each family. Knowing which family the part belongs to converts an anonymous mark into a named, expected discontinuity — or flags the mark that does *not* belong, which deserves the hardest look of all.

sections.caption: Shape is the first read: each discontinuity family leaves a characteristic signature in the developer.

sections.heading: Appearance: Reading Shape and Strength
sections.body:
Indication appearance sorts into a handful of repeatable signatures:

- **Continuous linear** — cracks bleed as **jagged, irregular lines**; cold shuts as **smooth, narrow, curving lines**; seams as **straight lines** following the working direction; laps as gently **curved lines**, lying at a shallow angle to the surface.
- **Intermittent linear** — a linear flaw that is partly closed shows only where it is still open. Peening, machining smear, grinding, and plating all close sections of an opening, so a broken line of dashes deserves the same suspicion as a solid one.
- **Rounded** — gas porosity and pits bleed as round, well-defined spots. A deep crack can also bleed so heavily that it rounds over into a blob — heavy bleed-out means volume, whatever the shape.
- **Fine dots** — a cluster of pinpoint indications is the signature of **microshrinkage** or fine porosity in castings.
- **Diffuse or weak** — a faint haze with no defined shape. It may be a wide shallow flaw, or it may be residual background from imperfect removal. Never interpret a haze: reclean and reprocess.

Strength scales with trapped volume: under UV-A a Type I indication's **brightness**, and in white light a Type II indication's **depth of red**, both rise with the amount of penetrant feeding the mark.

sections.caption: Field guide: typical PT behavior by discontinuity — typical responses; the written procedure and acceptance criteria govern.
rows: Thin, jagged continuous line at a stress riser
Slow — strengthens through the full window
High — redevelops quickly after a wipe
Bold, jagged, often forked line at fillets, holes, section changes
Network or parallel set of fine, sharp, shallow lines
Low — little volume to refeed the mark
Smooth, narrow, gently curving continuous line
Straight (seam) or curved (lap) line, often intermittent
Immediate — blooms as the developer dries
High for deep pores — fast re-bleed
Cluster of pinpoint dots — a spongy patch

sections.heading: Time to Appear: The Clock Is a Gauge
sections.body:
Bleed-out speed is a measurement. The wider the mouth and the larger the reservoir, the faster penetrant returns to the surface:

- **Immediate and strong** — gross porosity, deep open cracks, large pits. The indication blooms as the developer film dries.
- **Slow and building** — tight flaws over real depth: fatigue cracks, narrow-mouthed quench cracks, tight seams. The mark may be barely visible at two minutes and unmistakable at ten.
- **Quick, faint, then stalled** — shallow scratches, broad shallow conditions, and removal residue. Whatever volume existed is spent in the first moments.

Two practical rules follow. First, **watch parts during the first minutes of development** whenever the job allows — speed of appearance is interpretation data that no later observation can recover. Second, **respect both ends of the development window**. Reading early undersizes exactly the flaws that matter most, the tight ones; reading late lets indications spread, merge, and blur. Typical procedures set a minimum on the order of 10 minutes and a definite maximum — the written procedure governs both.

sections.caption: Bleed-out speed separates flaw families: open, voluminous flaws bloom at once; tight, deep cracks build steadily; shallow conditions flash faint and stall.

sections.heading: Persistence: Indications That Come Back
sections.body:
Persistence is the indication's staying power — and your last diagnostic lever.

An indication keeps growing as long as its reservoir keeps feeding. Deep flaws produce **persistent** indications that strengthen and survive the whole window. Shallow conditions produce marks that stall early and can even **fade** as their small charge of penetrant spreads thin through the developer. Fluorescent indications also dim if the dye is abused — prolonged UV-A exposure and excessive heat both degrade fluorescence — so a part parked under the booth lamp all afternoon no longer reads the way it did at ten minutes.

The classic persistence test is the **redevelopment check**: lightly wipe the indication away with a solvent-dampened swab and watch the spot.

- **It returns fast and strong** — there is volume below. The discontinuity is real and has depth.
- **It returns weakly, or not at all** — little or nothing remains below: a shallow condition or surface residue.

Use the check sparingly. Every wipe removes penetrant and the solvent dilutes what is left — one check, perhaps two, is the limit before the evidence itself is gone. If doubt survives the check, there is only one honest answer: clean the part and reprocess from the beginning.

sections.title: Identity = shape + strength + speed + staying power
sections.body: No single cue convicts. A jagged line that builds slowly and redevelops after a wipe is a crack until proven otherwise; a faint patch that stalls and never returns is a surface or removal condition until proven otherwise — and in both directions the proof is **reprocessing**, never wishful thinking. Identify the discontinuity first; only then evaluate it against the acceptance criteria. Your written procedure governs the development window and any redevelopment practice.

interactive.title: Bleed-Out Behavior Predictor
interactive.intro: Four parts are coming out of the developer station. For each one, commit to a prediction — appearance, timing, or persistence — before the indication forms. Then see how the bleed-out behaves.

steps.prompt: A landing-gear link with service time arrives for fluorescent PT; engineering suspects a fatigue crack at a machined radius. Nonaqueous developer has just flashed dry. What do you expect at the radius, and when?
options: A broad rounded glow the instant the developer dries
A thin, jagged line that starts faint and keeps strengthening through the development window
A cluster of pinpoint dots appearing after about a minute
Nothing until the part is rewarmed to drive the penetrant out
steps.feedback: A service fatigue crack is a tight mouth over real depth — a reservoir that drains slowly. The indication starts faint and builds for the entire development period, which is exactly why reading the part early undersizes the most dangerous flaw on the bench. Heat is not what drives bleed-out; the developer's blotting action is.

steps.prompt: Next: the machined face of a sand casting with suspected gas porosity. How will those indications behave?
options: Round, well-defined spots that bloom almost immediately as the developer dries
Jagged lines that build slowly over ten minutes
A smooth continuous line curving across the face
A faint haze with no defined shape that fades within a minute
steps.feedback: Gas pores are open volumes with relatively generous mouths: penetrant returns fast, so rounded indications appear nearly at once. Shape says cavity; speed says wide mouth and easy volume. Slow-building jagged lines belong to tight cracks, not pores.

steps.prompt: On a weld toe, a strong linear indication sits in a congested area of bleed-out. You lightly wipe it with a solvent-dampened swab and watch: within a minute, a crisp line re-forms in the same place. What did the redevelopment just tell you?
options: The indication was false — real indications never survive a wipe
The developer is contaminated and must be replaced
Nothing — redevelopment behavior is random
The discontinuity has real depth and volume — the reservoir below refilled the mark
steps.feedback: Wiping removes the surface mark, not the trapped penetrant. Fast, strong redevelopment means a reservoir with depth is re-feeding the developer — a significant discontinuity. Budget the technique: each wipe removes penetrant and the solvent dilutes what remains, so one check — perhaps two — is the limit.

steps.prompt: Last part: a broad, faint, fuzzy patch on a casting boss. It appeared quickly, stopped growing, and after a careful wipe it never returns. Your call as the Level II?
options: Reject the part — faint diffuse patches are cracks by definition
Treat it as a probable shallow surface condition or removal residue — and if any doubt remains, clean and fully reprocess before disposition
Accept the part immediately — faint always means harmless
Extend development by several hours until the patch returns
steps.feedback: Quick, faint, stalled, and no re-bleed adds up to a tiny or nonexistent reservoir: a broad shallow condition or imperfect removal — not a tight, deep flaw. But interpretation is never a guess in either direction: a questionable indication means reprocess, and disposition comes only from the acceptance criteria.

checkQuestions.question: On a thin wall of an aluminum sand casting, PT shows a smooth, narrow, continuous indication curving gently across the surface. Which discontinuity fits that signature?
options: Cold shut
Fatigue crack
Gas porosity
Grinding checks
checkQuestions.explanation: Two streams of metal that met too cold to fuse leave a smooth, narrow, curving line — and thin walls far from the gate are where metal arrives coldest. A crack would read jagged, porosity rounded, and grinding checks as a network of fine sharp lines.

checkQuestions.question: Two linear indications on the same part are equal in length, but one is wide and bright while the other is thin and faint. What primarily drives that difference?
options: The color of the penetrant used
The form of developer applied
The volume of penetrant trapped in each discontinuity
The intensity of the UV-A lamp
checkQuestions.explanation: Bleed-out — and therefore indication width and brightness — scales with the trapped volume, which is set by each flaw's depth and mouth width. Penetrant color, developer form, and lamp intensity act on both indications equally; only the reservoirs differ.

checkQuestions.question: A forging lap shows as an intermittent, broken line rather than a continuous one. What is the most likely reason?
options: The lap fused back together during heat treatment
Machining or other working smeared metal over parts of the opening, so penetrant entered only where it remains open
Intermittent lines are produced only by porosity
The developer film was applied too thin
checkQuestions.explanation: Peening, machining smear, grinding, and plating can close sections of a linear opening. Bleed-out occurs only at the still-open segments, so the indication breaks into dashes — and it deserves the same suspicion as a continuous line.

checkQuestions.question: Which behavior most strongly suggests a large-volume discontinuity with a wide-open mouth, such as gross porosity?
options: A faint line that appears late in the window
No indication until the part is re-rinsed
A mark that grows slowly for the full development time
A strong indication blooming almost immediately as the developer dries
checkQuestions.explanation: Bleed-out speed tracks mouth width and reservoir volume. Wide-open, voluminous flaws return penetrant the moment the developer film forms; slow, steady growth is the signature of tight, deep flaws instead.

checkQuestions.question: You lightly wipe a faint linear indication with a solvent-dampened swab and it does not redevelop. What should you conclude and do?
options: The flaw is deep and dangerous — reject the part
The indication was false — accept the part as is
Little or no penetrant remained below the surface — likely a shallow condition or residue; if doubt remains, clean and reprocess before any disposition
Spray penetrant directly on the spot and read it again in one minute
checkQuestions.explanation: No re-bleed means no significant reservoir — typical of shallow conditions or removal residue. But absence of redevelopment is not proof of acceptability by itself, and local re-application of penetrant is not a valid retest: when doubt remains, the part is cleaned and fully reprocessed.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.1 (Level II: reason for, appearance, time to appear, and persistence of indications)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (development and interpretation, general reference)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (interpretation guidance, general reference)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (general reference)
